input.query_abstract: The Internet, as well as many other networks, has a very complex connectivity recently modeled by the class of scale-free networks. This feature, which appears to be very efficient for a communications network, favors at the same time the spreading of computer viruses. We analyze real data from computer virus infections and find the average lifetime and prevalence of viral strains on the Internet. We define a dynamical model for the spreading of infections on scale-free networks, finding the absence of an epidemic threshold and its associated critical behavior. This new epidemiological framework rationalize data of computer viruses and could help in the understanding of other spreading phenomena on communication and social networks.
input.hyp_generic_object: a novel linguistic variant diffusing across a speaker interaction network
input.hyp_abstract: Linguistic innovations - a new pronunciation, a reanalysed construction, a borrowed discourse marker - spread from speaker to speaker through the fabric of everyday interaction. That fabric is highly unequal: a few socially central speakers converse with very many interlocutors, while most have a modest circle. We model how this inequality in interaction connectivity conditions whether an incipient variant catches on or fades. Each speaker is treated as currently favouring the innovative or the conservative variant, with the innovative form transmitted through interaction and speakers occasionally reverting, and we write mean-field equations for the innovating fraction within each connectivity class. When interaction is homogeneous, the variant propagates community-wide only if its per-interaction transmissibility - its social or communicative advantage - exceeds a positive critical value; below it the innovation dies out, offering a mechanism for failed changes. When the distribution of interlocutor counts is heavy-tailed and its variance diverges with community size, that critical advantage falls to zero: even a variant with negligible intrinsic advantage stabilises at a finite frequency across the community, kept alive by the most connected speakers who continually re-seed it. Iterating the equations reproduces the characteristic rise from zero without a threshold and predicts anomalous frequency scaling near the actuation point. The account suggests that the connectivity structure of a speech community, not merely the prestige of a form, can determine which innovations actuate and diffuse.
input.candidate_title: Triadic closure as a basic generating mechanism of communities in complex networks
input.candidate_abstract: Most of the complex social, technological and biological networks have a significant community structure. Therefore the community structure of complex networks has to be considered as a universal property, together with the much explored small-world and scale-free properties of these networks. Despite the large interest in characterizing the community structures of real networks, not enough attention has been devoted to the detection of universal mechanisms able to spontaneously generate networks with communities. Triadic closure is a natural mechanism to make new connections, especially in social networks. Here we show that models of network growth based on simple triadic closure naturally lead to the emergence of community structure, together with fat-tailed distributions of node degree, high clustering coefficients. Communities emerge from the initial stochastic heterogeneity in the concentration of links, followed by a cycle of growth and fragmentation. Communities are the more pronounced, the sparser the graph, and disappear for high values of link density and randomness in the attachment procedure. By introducing a fitness-based link attractivity for the nodes, we find a novel phase transition, where communities disappear for high heterogeneity of the fitness distribution, but a new mesoscopic organization of the nodes emerges, with groups of nodes being shared between just a few superhubs, which attract most of the links of the system.
